name: Concrete Patio Project
description: Plan your sub-base, reinforcement, and poured concrete slab from start to finish.

timeline.description: Excavate the site to the required depth, rake the soil flat, lay down weed fabric, and compact the gravel base.
Build wooden form boundaries, secure stakes, and lay down rebar grids with lap splices elevated on chairs.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: Hire Pro (High risk of concrete quick-setting)
timeline.description: Pour wet concrete mix, screed the top flat, float the surface, cut control joints, and apply broom finish.
Mist the slab with water regularly and cover with plastic sheeting to ensure optimal curing density.
Concrete reaches its full structural design strength. You may place heavy furniture or structures.

mistakes: Pouring concrete directly over soft soil without a compacted gravel base (causes sinking/cracking).
Forgetting to place rebar on chairs (rebar resting on the ground provides zero tensile strength).
Pouring concrete in direct scorching sun or sub-freezing temperatures without weather protection.
Adding excessive water to the concrete drum to make it easier to pour, which ruins the concrete's strength.

accessoryTools.name: Tie wire and wire twister tool
Concrete screed board and bull float
Safety goggles, heavy gloves, and rubber boots

preparationSteps: Call 811 to mark underground utilities before excavating
Check weather forecast — no rain expected for 48 hours after concrete pour
Order concrete delivery or confirm bag quantity for pickup
Arrange 3-4 helpers for pour day

items: Slope graded away from house at 1/4 inch per foot minimum?
Soil compacted thoroughly before gravel layer?
Weed barrier fabric laid over prepared base?
Forms level and braced against concrete pressure?
Rebar elevated on chairs — not resting on ground?
Expansion joint material placed where slab meets house?
Concrete slump within spec (not overwatered)?
Screed board long enough to span form width?
Control joint locations marked every 4-6 feet?

cleanupSteps: Remove forms after 24-48 hours once concrete has set
Dispose of excess concrete — never wash residue into storm drains
Clean tools before concrete hardens on them
Sweep and dispose of form debris and scrap lumber
maintenanceTips: Seal concrete every 2-3 years to prevent staining and freeze-thaw damage
Re-apply caulk at expansion joints and slab/house interface annually
Avoid de-icing salts — use sand or kitty litter for winter traction
Power wash gently — keep pressure below 1500 psi to avoid surface pitting

name: Backyard Shed Construction
description: Estimate foundation slab concrete, wall framing lumber, and roof shingles.

timeline.description: Prepare site, excavate, set form boards, and pour the concrete pad foundation.
Lay treated base plates, align floor joists, and screw down exterior-grade subfloor plywood sheets.
Frame and erect the four walls, framing door/window headers, and bracing corners plumb.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: Hire Pro or DIY (Heights and structural load hazard)
timeline.description: Cut and place roof rafters or trusses, nail down plywood sheathing panels.
Install weather barriers, shingles, siding panels, trim boards, windows, and doors.

mistakes: Using non-treated wood in direct contact with concrete slab foundation (leads to rot).
Failing to check that wall frames are perfectly square and plumb before attaching sheathing.
Inadequate roof pitch causing rainwater ponding behind shingles.
Omitting subfloor ventilation gaps leading to mold buildup beneath floor boards.

accessoryTools.name: Galvanized framing nails and deck screws

preparationSteps: Check local zoning — sheds over 120 sq ft may require permit
Call 811 to mark underground utilities before digging footings
Verify shed site is level and well-drained
Order materials delivery — concrete, lumber, roofing

items: Slab forms level and squared to layout lines?
Rebar grid on chairs at correct height?
Anchor bolts placed in wet concrete per plan?
Bottom plate pressure-treated and on sill seal?
Walls plumb, square, and temporarily braced?
Header spans correct for door/window openings?
Roof sheathing gap 1/8 inch for expansion?
Ice and water shield at eaves and valleys?
Flashing installed at roof-to-wall intersections?

cleanupSteps: Remove construction debris and scrap lumber from site
Return unused roofing materials for refund within return window
Store leftover paint and sealant in conditioned space
Sweep sawdust and nails from surrounding area
maintenanceTips: Inspect roof annually for loose or damaged shingles
Re-apply exterior stain or paint every 3-5 years
Check for ground moisture wicking into base plate each spring
Keep gutters clean and downspouts extending 4 ft from foundation

name: Roof Shingle Replacement
description: Calculate pitch, deck underlayment, sheathing sheets, and shingle bundles.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: Hire Pro (Falls risk and heavy debris management)
timeline.description: Remove existing asphalt shingles, valley metals, flashings, and old felt paper down to wood deck.
Inspect underlying plywood decking for water damage, rot, or sagging; replace boards as needed.
Install ice & water shield at eaves and valleys, lay synthetic underlayment over roof fields.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: Hire Pro (Requires exact nailing pattern to hold warranty)
timeline.description: Install metal drip edges, nail down starter shingle rows, and lay main shingle fields.
Install ridge vents, flashing collars on plumbing vents, and nail down ridge cap shingles.

mistakes: Layering new shingles directly over old, curled shingles (reduces shingle life and voids warranties).
Nailing shingles incorrectly (high nailing or using too few nails causes shingles to blow off).
Re-using rusted flashings or damaged valley linings to save cost.
Improper ridge vent spacing causing condensation buildup in the attic.

accessoryTools.name: Pneumatic coil nail gun and compressor

preparationSteps: Check forecast — no rain or high winds for 5 consecutive days
Confirm roof deck condition — replace rotted sheathing before shingling
Rent dumpster or arrange bag-based debris disposal
Order shingles, underlayment, and flashing — allow 2-week lead for special colors

items: All gutters and downspouts protected from debris?
Drop cloths covering landscaping and walkways?
Trash chute or dumpster positioned safely?
Ice and water shield extends 6 ft up from eave?
Synthetic underlayment overlapped 6 inches minimum?
Valleys and pipe penetrations flashed before underlayment?
Starter strip installed with 1/2 inch overhang at eave?
First shingle course offset 6 inches from starter?
Nails placed in nailing zone (not too high, not too low)?

cleanupSteps: Remove all old shingle debris from roof deck and gutters
Magnetic sweeper over driveway and lawn to collect roofing nails
Dispose of tear-off debris per local landfill guidelines
Return unused shingle bundles within supplier return window
maintenanceTips: Inspect roof after major storms for missing or damaged shingles
Clean gutters at least twice per year — spring and fall
Check attic for light penetration or water stains after heavy rain
Trim overhanging tree branches to prevent moss and debris accumulation

name: Deck Construction Project
description: Design your deck from the ground up — footings, framing, decking boards, railings, and finish.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: Hire Pro (Post-hole digging and concrete pouring)
timeline.description: Mark post locations, dig pier holes below frost line, pour concrete footings and set post anchors.
Install ledger board, beam supports, and joist hangers. Ensure level and square framing.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: DIY (Use deck screw gun for speed)
timeline.description: Screw down deck boards with proper gap spacing for drainage and expansion.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: DIY (Follow IRC code for baluster spacing)
timeline.description: Install posts, top and bottom rails, balusters at 4-inch max spacing, and build stair stringers.
Clean deck surface, apply weather-resistant stain or sealant, and allow 24-hour dry time.

mistakes: Setting posts below frost line in cold climates (causes frost heave and deck shifting).
Using untreated lumber for ground-contact posts and beams (leads to rot within 2-3 years).
Forgetting to install joist tape or flashing on top of joists (prevents rot from standing water).
Over-spanning joists beyond code limits per the IRC span table, causing bounce.

accessoryTools.name: Circular saw and deck screw gun
Deck stain and sealer (1 gallon per 200 sq ft)

preparationSteps: Call 811 to mark underground utilities before digging post holes
Verify local building department requirements — footings below frost line
Submit deck permit if required by local code (typically required for decks over 30 inches high)
Order lumber and hardware — pressure-treated, joist hangers, ledger flashing

items: Post holes dug below frost line (check local code depth)?
Sonotubes plumb and braced before concrete pour?
Post anchors set level and at correct spacing?
Ledger board bolted to house with proper flashing behind it?
Joist hangers all sized correctly and fully nailed?
Beam spans within code limits per IRC table?
Deck boards gapped 1/8 inch for drainage and expansion?
Screws driven flush — not countersunk below surface?
Joist tape applied to top of all joists?

cleanupSteps: Remove all construction debris, sawdust, and fasteners from site
Dispose of concrete washout water properly — never into storm drains
Return unused hardware, stain, and lumber within return window
Store leftover stain/sealer in conditioned space for future touch-ups
maintenanceTips: Clean and re-seal deck surface every 1-2 years depending on sun exposure
Check ledger board flashing annually for signs of water intrusion
Tighten loose railings and balusters each spring after freeze-thaw cycle
Inspect posts at ground level for rot or insect damage yearly

name: Fence Installation Project
description: Plan a wood or vinyl fence with posts, rails, pickets, and concrete footings.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: DIY (Use string line and marking paint)
timeline.description: Locate property lines, mark post positions at 6-8 foot intervals, and call 811 for utility locate.

timeline.hiringRecommendation: DIY (Rent power auger for hard soil)
timeline.description: Dig post holes 24-36 inches deep, set posts in concrete, and brace plumb until concrete cures.
Attach horizontal rails between posts, then install pickets with consistent spacing using a jig.
Build or install gate frame, hang on heavy-duty hinges, and apply final stain or weather treatment.

mistakes: Failing to call 811 before digging (hitting buried gas or electrical lines is dangerous and costly).
Setting posts too shallow (less than 24 inches) — causes gate sag and fence leaning over time.
Not allowing concrete to cure 24-48 hours before attaching rails (shifts post alignment).
Installing pickets without a bottom gap — leads to rot from ground moisture contact.

accessoryTools.name: Pneumatic nailer or deck screw gun

preparationSteps: Locate property lines — survey recommended if unsure of boundaries
Call 811 to mark underground utilities before digging any post holes
Check HOA or local rules on fence height, set-back, and materials
Order fence materials and concrete for post footings

items: Post holes 24-36 inches deep (below frost line in cold climates)?
Posts spaced at consistent intervals (typically 6-8 ft)?
Each post plumb in both directions before concrete sets?
Top and bottom rails level across each section?
Picket spacing consistent — use a jig for repeatability?
Picket tops aligned with string line?
Gate opening measured diagonally for square?
Gate hinges rated for gate weight?
Stain or sealant applied to all exposed wood surfaces?

cleanupSteps: Remove excess concrete, stakes, and form debris from post holes
Sweep nails, screws, and sawdust from work area
Return unused fencing materials and hardware within return window
Store leftover stain or paint for future fence repairs
maintenanceTips: Re-stain or seal wood fence every 2-3 years to prevent weather damage
Check gate hinges and latch annually — lubricate moving parts
Replace any warped or rotted pickets immediately to prevent spread
Keep vegetation cleared 6 inches from fence line to reduce moisture contact

name: Driveway Construction Project
description: Estimate sub-base gravel, concrete slab volume, and surface area for a residential driveway.

timeline.description: Excavate to required sub-base depth (typically 8-12 inches), grade for drainage slope of 1/4 inch per foot.
Lay and compact gravel base in 4-inch lifts, wetting each layer for maximum density.
Set forms along driveway edges, place 1/2-inch rebar in a 24-inch grid pattern on chairs.
Pour, screed, float, and broom-finish concrete. Cut control joints every 8-10 feet.
Keep concrete moist for 7 days minimum. No vehicle traffic for 7 days, full load after 28 days.

mistakes: Insufficient sub-base compaction causing slab cracking under vehicle weight.
Pouring concrete thinner than 5 inches for standard passenger vehicle driveways (minimum 5 inches).
Forgetting to slope the driveway for drainage — water pooling accelerates freeze-thaw damage.
Cutting control joints too shallow or too late — uncontrolled cracking occurs.
preparationSteps: Call 811 to locate underground utilities before any excavation
Verify driveway slope for proper drainage — minimum 1/4 inch per foot away from garage
Check if permit is required — driveways typically need a permit for curb-cut and drainage
Order gravel, rebar, and concrete or asphalt — schedule ready-mix delivery

items: Excavated 8-12 inches deep for sub-base + concrete thickness?
Slope graded at 1/4 inch per foot away from structure?
Compacted subgrade with plate compactor — no soft spots?
Gravel placed in 4-inch lifts and compacted between each lift?
Base thickness meets local code for driveway loads?
Base surface level and sloped correctly before rebar?
Forms set to correct finished grade with 5-6 inch slab thickness?
Rebar grid on chairs in center of slab thickness?
Control joints planned every 8-10 feet for 10x10 sections?

cleanupSteps: Remove forms after 24-48 hours once concrete has cured sufficiently
Clean tools and equipment before concrete residue hardens
Dispose of excess gravel and concrete waste properly
Seal control joints with self-leveling sealant after full cure
maintenanceTips: Seal concrete driveway every 2-3 years to protect from oil stains and freeze-thaw damage
Fill cracks with concrete repair caulk as soon as they appear to prevent water infiltration
Re-apply sealant to control joints annually before winter
Avoid de-icing chemicals — use sand for traction to prevent surface spalling

name: Room Addition Project
description: Calculate framing, drywall, paint, tile, and flooring for a new room addition.

timeline.description: Pour foundation, frame walls, install roof trusses or rafters, and sheath exterior.
Run wiring, outlets, switches, and plumbing lines through framed walls before insulation.
Install insulation, hang and tape drywall, apply joint compound and sand smooth.
Install tile or flooring, baseboards, door casings, and window trim.
Prime and paint walls and ceiling, install light fixtures and switch plates, final cleanup.

mistakes: Skipping the permit and inspection process (unpermitted additions are flagged during home sales).
Not insulating exterior walls to local code R-value requirements (leads to high energy costs).
Placing electrical boxes too shallow for final drywall thickness (code violation).
Installing flooring before drywall finishing is complete — dust and debris damage new floors.

accessoryTools.name: Drywall taping knives and mud pan
Safety dust mask and drop cloths

preparationSteps: Obtain building permit — room additions always require permits and inspections
Verify foundation meets local frost depth requirements
Hire licensed trades for electrical and plumbing rough-in
Order materials in phases — foundation first, then framing, then finishes

items: Foundation poured to correct height above grade?
Framing inspected by building department before closing walls?
All rough openings sized correctly for windows and doors?
Insulation R-value meets local code requirements for your zone?
Vapor barrier installed on warm side of wall?
All electrical boxes extended to final drywall thickness?
Flooring allowed to acclimate in room for 48-72 hours before installation?
Paint primer applied before color coats?
Trim caulked and nail holes filled before final paint?

cleanupSteps: Dispose of construction debris — drywall dust, scrap lumber, packaging
HVAC filters replaced after all drywall sanding is complete
Final clean — wipe all surfaces, vacuum floors, clean windows
Return unused flooring, tile, and paint within supplier return window
maintenanceTips: Check for settling cracks in drywall after first year — patch as needed
Keep gutters clean to prevent water infiltration near new foundation
Monitor humidity levels in new room — consider dehumidifier if above 60%
Re-caulk trim, baseboards, and window casings as seasonal movement occurs

name: Exterior Wall & Siding Project
description: Plan a retaining wall, brick veneer, siding installation, or board-and-batten accent wall.

timeline.description: Remove old siding or clean wall surface, repair any rot or damage, install weather barrier and flashing.
Install vertical furring strips for drainage plane, attach starter strips for siding or brick ledge.
Install siding panels or brick veneer, overlapping correctly and leaving expansion gaps.
Install corner trim, J-channels around windows and doors, and apply final caulking.

mistakes: Installing siding without a proper drainage plane behind it (trapped moisture causes rot).
Nailing siding too tight — thermal expansion causes buckling in summer heat.
Not leaving weepholes at the bottom of brick veneer (trapped water destroys mortar).
Mixing siding materials without proper transition flashing between different wall claddings.
preparationSteps: Inspect existing wall sheathing for rot, water damage, or insect infestation
Remove old siding, shutters, and trim — dispose of debris per local guidelines
Install temporary weather protection if rain is expected during multi-day project
Order siding material, flashing, trim, and fasteners — allow 1-2 weeks for special orders

items: All rot and damage repaired before weather barrier installed?
Old flashing removed and replaced — not covered over?
Wall surface clean, dry, and smooth before barrier?
Weather barrier lapped correctly (top over bottom)?
Siding nailed at proper angle and depth per manufacturer spec?
Expansion gaps left at ends and around windows/doors?
J-channels installed around all windows and doors?
Weep holes at bottom of brick or stone veneer?
All exposed nail heads sealed with color-matched caulk?

cleanupSteps: Remove old siding debris, nails, and construction waste from property
Magnetic sweeper to collect dropped nails and screws from lawn
Clean caulk nozzles and store sealant for future repairs
Return unused siding panels and trim within supplier return window
maintenanceTips: Inspect siding annually for cracks, gaps, or loose panels after winter freeze-thaw
Power wash vinyl or fiber cement siding every 2-3 years with gentle pressure
Re-caulk around windows, doors, and corner trim as sealant degrades over time
Keep shrubs and vegetation trimmed at least 6 inches from siding to prevent moisture damage
